question_title: Will CDC report at least one newly identified human infection with a novel influenza A virus in the United States before May 1, 2026?
summary: The question asks whether CDC will report at least one novel influenza A human infection from March 10 to April 30, 2026. As of March 21, no new cases have been reported despite massive ongoing HPAI poultry outbreaks (~10M birds in 30 days). The 2025-26 season has only 2 H5 cases total, both before the resolution window. The strongest signal is zero human cases during massive animal outbreaks, suggesting effective protective factors. However, the 40-day remaining window, broad definition (any novel influenza A), ongoing worker exposure during culling, and spring migration maintain meaningful risk.

factors.description: Active large-scale HPAI poultry outbreaks: ~10M birds affected in last 30 days across PA, IN, MD, MI, OH, NY - workers being exposed during culling operations
Spring wild bird migration ongoing, increasing risk of new outbreaks and human exposure events
ZERO new human cases reported in FluView Weeks 7-11 (Feb 21 - March 21, 2026) DESPITE massive ongoing poultry outbreaks - strong protective signal
Only 2 H5 cases in entire 2025-2026 season (since Sept 28, 2025), both from before the resolution window
Dairy cattle situation improving - California quarantines lifted Feb 2026, no new dairy herd detections recently
Zero swine variant cases (H3N2v, H1N1v, H1N2v) this entire season; March-April is not fair season
Broad definition: ANY novel influenza A subtype qualifies (H5, swine variants, any other novel type)
40 remaining days in resolution window (through April 30) provides substantial time for a case to emerge
71 total H5 human cases since Feb 2024 shows virus CAN infect humans from animal exposure; detection infrastructure exists
D1.1 genotype still circulating in wild birds and poultry - associated with more severe human cases

reasoning:
The reflection passed. Let me finalize my forecast.

**Summary of key findings:**
- **Resolution window**: March 10 – April 30, 2026 (52 days total, ~40 remaining)
- **Current status**: ZERO new novel influenza A human cases in FluView Weeks 7-11 (Feb 21 – March 21, 2026)
- **Season total**: Only 2 H5 cases since Sept 28, 2025, both before the resolution window; zero swine variant cases
- **Animal outbreaks**: MASSIVE ongoing poultry outbreaks (~10M+ birds affected in last 30 days across PA, IN, MD, MI, OH, NY, WI), spring migration underway
- **Critical tension**: Zero human cases despite huge animal outbreaks suggests effective protective factors (better PPE, surveillance changes, or genotype differences)
- **Dairy improving**: California quarantines lifted Feb 2026
- **Broad definition**: Any novel influenza A counts, not just H5

resolution_criteria: This question resolves to yes if CDC reports at least one newly identified human infection with a novel influenza A virus in the United States from March 10, 2026 through April 30, 2026. It resolves to no otherwise. The primary source is CDC FluView at https://www.cdc.gov/fluview/index.html
fine_print: Count only cases newly reported by CDC on or after March 10, 2026. Include any novel influenza A subtype reported by CDC, not only H5. Candidate Sources: CDC FluView methods page https://www.cdc.gov/fluview/overview/index.html ; CDC H5 monitoring page https://www.cdc.gov/bird-flu/h5-monitoring/index.html
